task: I’m trying to sort out a shipment that arrived short and the supplier is pushing back on the invoice. Can you help me figure out the cleanest way to document the discrepancy and what I should ask for before I agree to pay anything else?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it tight and document-first.

### What to record
Make a simple discrepancy log with:
- **PO / order number**
- **Invoice number**
- **Delivery date**
- **What was ordered**
- **What arrived**
- **Short quantity / missing items**
- **Condition on arrival** if relevant
- **Who received it**
- **Photos / count sheet / packing slip**
- **Any emails or calls about the short shipment**

### What to ask the supplier for
Before agreeing to any extra payment, ask for:
- **Their written explanation** of the short shipment
- **A revised invoice** showing exactly what they believe is still owed
- **The packing list / ship confirmation / bill of lading**
- **Any proof of delivery or count verification**
- **Their proposed correction**: resend, credit, or invoice adjustment

### Clean response you can send
> We received the shipment and noted a quantity discrepancy against the order and invoice. Please send the supporting paperwork for your position, including the packing list, delivery confirmation, and a revised invoice if applicable. Until the discrepancy is documented and resolved against the order records, we’re not able to approve any additional payment.

### Best practice
Don’t argue the amount informally. Keep everything tied to:
- the **written order**
- the **delivery paperwork**
- the **count you actually received**

If you want, I can turn this into a short email template or a one-line dispute log format.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. I’d tighten it up more and make it harder for them to wiggle out.

What I’d want added:
- **Exact count at receipt**. Not just “short.” Put the unit count on the log.
- **Who counted it and when**. If it was a blind receive, say that.
- **Carrier info / seal number** if there was one.
- **Photos of pallet labels, carton labels, and the count sheet**. Not just the goods.
- **Reference the contract terms** if they exist — shortage claim window, freight terms, dispute procedure.

What to ask for before paying anything else:
- written position on the shortage
- revised invoice tied to the exact units they say were delivered
- BOL / POD / packing list
- their **signed count** or warehouse verification, if they claim it left complete
- credit memo or replacement shipment plan, not just “we’ll look into it”

I’d also make the language a little harder:
> We received X units against Y ordered/invoiced. The discrepancy has been logged against the receiving record. Please provide the supporting shipping and delivery documents, along with a revised invoice or credit memo reflecting the shortage. No additional payment will be approved until the shortage is resolved in writing against the contract and receiving records.

That’s cleaner. Keeps it on paper, not a phone argument.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s the right direction. I’d make it even more audit-friendly and harder to dodge by separating **facts**, **evidence**, and **requested remedy**.

### Clean discrepancy log
Record this in one place:

- **PO / order number**
- **Supplier name**
- **Invoice number**
- **Delivery date / time**
- **Received by / counted by**
- **Count method**: blind receive or checked against paperwork
- **Ordered quantity**
- **Invoiced quantity**
- **Actual received quantity**
- **Short quantity**
- **Carrier name**
- **Tracking / PRO number**
- **Seal number** if applicable
- **Condition on arrival**
- **Packing slip / BOL / POD reference**
- **Photos saved**: pallet labels, carton labels, count sheet, damage if any
- **Who was notified and when**

### What to ask for before paying anything else
Ask for these in writing:

1. **Their written position** on the shortage  
2. **Revised invoice** tied to the exact units they say were delivered  
3. **Packing list / BOL / POD**  
4. **Their warehouse or ship count verification** if they claim it left complete  
5. **Credit memo, replacement plan, or corrected billing**  
6. **Any contract clause they rely on** for shortage claims, claim window, or freight terms

### Stronger response you can send
> We received **X units** against **Y ordered/invoiced**. The discrepancy has been logged in our receiving record, including count details, date/time, receiver, carrier information, and supporting photos. Please provide your written position, the BOL/POD/packing list, and either a revised invoice, credit memo, or replacement plan reflecting the shortage. No additional payment will be approved until the discrepancy is resolved in writing against the contract and receiving records.

### If you want to be even firmer
Add:
> If you believe the shipment left complete, please provide your signed count or warehouse verification supporting that position.

If you want, I can turn this into a **short email**, a **discrepancy log template**, or a **supplier reply that sounds firmer but still professional**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s solid. The split into facts, evidence, remedy is the part that matters. Harder to hand-wave.

I’d only make two small tweaks:
- put the **exact shortage math** in the first line: ordered / invoiced / received / short
- keep the last sentence tied to **approval**, not debate. Less room for them to play dumb.

That wording is good enough to use as-is.
